segments.title: An American naval campaign under pressure
segments.summary: The campaign opens with the United States behind in technology, rich on paper but facing several wars and a difficult capital-ship programme.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames the American position as a balance between financial capacity, technological lag, and immediate wartime commitments against Germany, Austria, and Spain. The early decisions are therefore not simply about winning a battle: he must choose where to send capital ships, which islands and ports to target, and how to turn economic resources into a more capable fleet. It is a useful watch point for seeing how force structure and strategy constrain each other in a naval campaign.

evidence.note: The United States is technologically behind, at war with Germany, Austria, and Spain, and sends battleships toward the Marshall Islands.

segments.title: Pursuing battlecruisers through machinery research
segments.summary: A bid for battlecruisers makes cruiser design and improved engines central to the American research programme, while a 21-knot battleship is treated as fast for 1900.
segments.body: The stream links capital-ship ambitions to the less glamorous work of machinery and cruiser-design research. While battlecruisers remain out of reach, Dr. Clarke develops a fast battleship by shedding weight and balancing armour, guns, and speed. He notes that 21 knots counts as a fast battleship in the campaign's 1900 setting, illustrating how a design judgement depends on period expectations rather than a modern benchmark.

evidence.note: He wants cruiser design because it is the route to battlecruisers and welcomes improved engines.
Weight is traded among deck and belt protection to produce a 21-knot fast battleship for 1900.

segments.title: Funding economic growth before fleet expansion
segments.summary: The American campaign spends heavily on the economy and merchant marine first, accepting an early financial burden to unlock later naval spending.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains his campaign's financial logic as front-loading investment rather than treating every monthly deficit as failure. In this model, subsidising the economy and putting money into the merchant marine expands GDP; once that base is established, sustained naval spending becomes far easier. The moment connects fleet building to the industrial and commercial resources that must support it.

evidence.note: He says early American spending should boost GDP and the merchant marine, after which funds become much easier to sustain.

segments.title: Designing light cruisers for mine warfare
segments.summary: Port limits and mine-laying requirements push the campaign toward a compact, fast light-cruiser design rather than another battleship.
segments.body: At a port that cannot take a battleship, Dr. Clarke works through a smaller cruiser option and prioritises mine-laying capability. The resulting concept is described as an 8,500-ton, 24-knot ship resembling an early heavy destroyer, but its practical purpose is to lay minefields. This is a clear example of a mission requirement shaping displacement, speed, and armament choices.

evidence.note: A 5,000-ton port limit rules out a battleship; he develops a cruiser design with mine-laying capability.
He states that the ships are intended for minefields.

segments.title: Turning the Caribbean into a minefield
segments.summary: New light cruisers are commissioned to mine a large maritime area and make the American-controlled Caribbean dangerous for opponents to enter.
segments.body: The earlier design work becomes an operational plan here. Dr. Clarke intends to use the light cruisers to seed the Caribbean with mines, turning control of surrounding waters into a form of sea denial. The emphasis is not on finding an enemy fleet for a decisive battle, but on making movement through a strategically important region costly and uncertain.

evidence.note: The new light cruisers will begin mining large bodies of ocean, with the Caribbean envisioned as one large minefield.

segments.title: How Age-of-Sail blockades stayed supplied
segments.question: Could sailing warships remain on station for long periods without resupply?
segments.answerShort: They could remain at sea, but required a steady flow of food, water, fuel, powder, and ammunition. Small Royal Navy vessels carried supplies, and blockading forces often bought provisions locally, including from French sources during the blockade of France.
segments.summary: A question about endurance at sea becomes an explanation of the supply system that kept Age-of-Sail blockades operating.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke distinguishes between a ship staying on station and a ship operating without resupply. Routine gunnery practice alone consumed powder and ammunition, while food, fresh water, and fuel for cooking also had to reach the fleet. He points to brigs, cutters, and other small vessels as the logistics network behind the Royal Navy's blockades. The answer also highlights the paradox that British blockaders could buy food from French sources, using superior purchasing power in ways that could raise local prices and worsen shortages.

evidence.note: The answer challenges the premise that ships could avoid resupply and lists ammunition, food, water, and cooking fuel.
He describes small Royal Navy supply vessels and says estimates put French-sourced food at roughly a quarter to a third of British blockade consumption.

segments.title: When technology and torpedo boats fail in battle
segments.summary: A battle exposes weak fire-control technology and the danger of torpedo boats leaving their screening role without useful orders.
segments.body: During the engagement, Dr. Clarke finds that sustained firing has produced no hits and links the problem to an earlier technological deficit. He characterises his battleship as a short-range platform whose twelve-inch guns only become effective at close distance. At the same time, torpedo boats have wandered away from the fight while on automatic control, so he returns them to a screening mission. The episode ties combat effectiveness to both technical quality and the control of lighter forces.

evidence.note: Poor accuracy is associated with technical shortcomings; the battleship is described as effective only at short range.
Torpedo boats have moved away from the battle on automatic control and are ordered back to screen the fleet.

segments.title: Balancing range, torpedoes, and secondary guns
segments.summary: A final fast-battleship design trades some range for a more balanced armament, adding torpedoes and four-inch guns alongside ten-inch weapons.
segments.body: The design discussion shows that a long-range specification can consume space and weight needed for other fighting qualities. Dr. Clarke reduces the planned range to a more practical figure, then adds torpedoes to discourage close approach and works to improve the secondary battery. The finished concept favours speed and a realistic mixed armament over maximum firepower, making the trade-offs in a capital-ship design visible.

evidence.note: The ship is a fast-battleship design with limited firepower; range is reduced and torpedoes are added.
The design adds four-inch guns while retaining ten-inch weapons and reaches a satisfactory weight balance.
